Hey, welcome back. Now, I took a quick break here from that interview. We're going to return there again, but before I do, there's a few things I want to discuss with you to kind of highlight some of the things I spoke about. And the first thing is I want to talk about the difference between vision and goals, because it's a huge one. Now, for starters, a vision is far more important than a goal for three core reasons. First number one, goals are temporary. They're essentially there and then they're not there. They come and they go. They're almost stopping points along the way. They're not forever. You set one, you achieve it, then you move on to the next one. In fact, sometimes you set one and you don't achieve it and you move on to the next one. It happens very, very often. See, you can't become too invested in your goals. You're almost just sort of stopping points along the way to determine whether you're headed towards your vision or not. Conversely, a vision is for the long term. It stays with you. It's something that guides your progress for year after year after year. Okay? It's not coming and going like a goal is. It's something that's visceral. It's in the future and it just simply is a guiding light to everything you do. Number two, goals don't inspire other people. You see, to get ahead in life, you typically need to enlist the support of other people around you. You need other people, essentially, to buy into your vision with their time and their money, their sweat equity, their elbow grease, right? But people will never buy into your goals, but they will buy into your vision. You see, every human being is thirsty for a vision and since so few people actually have one, they end up gravitating towards someone who has an inspiring vision. So once you become that visionary in your own life, you start to attract all these other vision thirsty people who will then buy into your vision and help you turn into reality. So again, if you're a serial goal setter with no vision on top of that, you're going to find yourself as a one person army moving through your own life, trying to achieve your vision and it's very, very hard. However, once you've truly become that visionary in your own life, bam, you're going to have those people just jumping on board thirsty for a vision and they'll be attracted to yours. And third, lastly, your goals are not destinations. They're merely stepping stones along the way to realizing your vision. Goals enable you to essentially achieve certain objectives. A vision, on the other hand, helps you realize your dreams. It starts with knowing where you want to go, having a target, having this compelling vision for your future. That's at the top, at the highest levels of the vision. Then beneath the vision, you have goals, which are short term. Your goals come and go. You don't always hit your goals. Listen, how many times have you set a goal, worked your tail off to achieve it and didn't? I have, right? So it's not like you're not so invested. Oh my God, I didn't get my goal. Oh my God, it's terrible. No, your goals come and go. It's your vision that's permanent. It's that vision that's where you're really focusing off in the long term. So you set a goal, which is almost like your stopping point along the way, and it lets you know whether you're moving towards your vision or away from your vision. But it's not the be all end, because here's what happens. If on the flip side, people have this way that they become goal oriented. And that's great. I'd rather be vision oriented. But if you're goal oriented to the point where you say, I can't be happy. Now, I'm working towards my goal. And when I finally hit my goal, then I'll be happy. And guess what happens? You ever set a goal and achieve it? You ever have? Of course you have, right? What do you do when you get there? You say, great, what's next? So now you end up moving. So you're happy for a second. You're like, what's next? So you end up being miserable all the time. You had a moment of happiness and a lifetime of misery, because you're just setting goals and so invested in the outcomes. I set my goals. And I then say, who do I have to align myself with to achieve my goals? What people will need to know, what organizations, what skills do I need to learn? I put a date on my goals. And I work towards my goals, but I don't become emotionally invested in my goals. My emotional investment is in my vision. My goals are the stopping points along the way. And I'm laser guided on my vision. But as I look at my vision out of the corner of my eyes, out of my peripheral vision, I'm always looking, analyzing opportunities along the way and saying, maybe I should look at that and maybe incorporate that into my vision. My vision is malleable. I said that right in the beginning. Your vision is constantly changing and evolving and growing, becoming better and more compelling as your own needs and your own, essentially, your desires change as you grow yourself as a human being. And then you learn your own lessons along the way. Your vision could very well change. My vision right now at 49 years old is not my vision. I assure you at 24 years old. Back then my vision was just money raining down on the sky. And I got it and realized it wasn't what it was cracked up to be. Now my vision is more about contribution. It's about my family. It's about really living my life at a very high ethical level. The money comes automatically when you're good at what you do, when you know the secrets to success, which is inner and outer game and being congruent, essentially having both of those worlds together. Understand that you have to essentially do both. You have to focus on the inner game and the outer game. The stuff that happens up here and the real world strategy is to get what you want. When you have that, you're going to make money. It's very easy to make money. But the idea is to live a happy, productive life while you're doing that and not to let it eat you alive, not to let it essentially get the best of you and let things like greed and greed supplant ambition, ambition, which is the healthy form of wanting good things for your life versus greed, which is about just getting as much as you want as fast as you want. And again, it all comes back to vision. It starts there creating that vision and then learning how to focus on that vision. You know what the enemy of great is in life? The enemy of great is good. Typically, the problem with most people and their goals is not that they set goals that are too high and miss them. The problem usually is they set their goals too low and they hit them. And because of that, oh, I'm feeling okay, I hit my goal and they end up at this place of mediocrity. And when you're in that place where you're not really feeling pain or when there's no pain, there's no impetus to change. So what happens with people who are creating a vision, the same thing. If they create a vision that really isn't all that inspiring because as they're creating the vision, say, well, I could never really own the big house. I don't really have what it takes to become a CEO or a sales manager or a top producer. They start doing their vision, such as writing out their vision saddled by all the limiting beliefs from the past. Not really realizing that they have just been handed, essentially in this case, they've been handed a gift. They've been handed a technique that can take them from being an average closer to being a world-class closer.
